coredata.dc:title: New detection method for nucleoside triphosphates based on carbon dots: The distance-dependent singlet oxygen trapping
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Ying
Jia, Qingyan
Wen, Yongmei
Liu, Weimin
Ge, Jiechao
Wu, Jiasheng
Zhang, Hongyan
Wang, Pengfei
coredata.dc:description: The distance-dependent based sensing mechanism, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption of gold nanoparticles, has been used widely in visual detection. In this work, we report another distance-dependent detection method for nucleoside triphosphates (NTPs) based on carbon dots (CDs) (1O2 donor) and 9, 10-diphenylanthracene-2-boronic acid (DABA, 1O2 acceptor). The CDs can generate singlet oxygen (1O2) which allows diffusion within 200 nm. Thus, the distance between CDs and DABA decreased through binding of NTPs (<200 nm), leading to absorption changes of DABA under light irradiation due to 1O2 trapping. This sensing system (CDs@DABA) has high selectivity for the detection of NTPs due to the double molecular recognition and a linear response in the 0–80 μM concentration range was accomplished with the detection limit as low as 4.35 μM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nucleoside triphosphates
Distance-dependent
Singlet oxygen trapping

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorbance (blank line) and emission (red line) spectra (λex = 450 nm) of CDs in distilled water (pH = 7.4). (b) TEM images of CDs. Scale bar, 20 nm. (c) Size distribution of the CDs (0.02 mg mL−1) in water. (d) Size distribution of the CDs (0.02 mg mL−1)@DABA (60 μM)-ATP (60 μM) in water. (e) Zeta potential of CDs (+22.6 mV). (f) Zeta potential of CDs (0.02 mg mL−1)@DABA (60 μM)-ATP (60 μM) (+4.91 mV). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Absorption changes of DABA (60 μM) in CDs solution (0.02 mg mL−1) (a) in the absence of ATP and (b) in the presence of ATP (60 μM) under white light (8 mW cm−2) measured every 20s within 5 min.

(a) The absorption decrease intensity ratio (ΔA/A0) of CDs@DABA after 5 min irradiation under white light at 387 nm upon different ATP concentrations. (b) The absorption decrease intensity ratio (ΔA/A0) of CDs@DABA after 5 min irradiation under white light at 387 nm for NTPs (60 μM).

(a) FT-IR spectra of the complex of CDs@DABA-ATP (black line), free CDs (red line), ATP (blue line) and DABA (green line), respectively. (b) Effect of pH on the absorption intensity changes at 387 nm of CDs@DABA (black line) and CDs@DABA-ATP (red line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Absorption response of DABA at 387 nm by adding equal amount of ATP, ADP, or AMP. (b) Absorption change ratio toward various analytes at 387 nm. Black bars represent the addition of a certain single substance (60 μM) to the CDs@DABA, and red bars represent the extra addition of ATP (60 μM) to each ion mixture. Each experiment was measured under white light irradiation for 5 min. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Absorption responses with different concentrations of ATP (0–80 μM) at 387 nm of CDs solution diluted by 1% FBS.
Sensing principle based on distance-dependent 1O2 trapping.
Schematic illustration in detecting ATP based on CDs@DABA.
Comparison of several typical methods used for ATP detection.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: There is no mentioning of application examples in article.

head.ce:title: New detection method for nucleoside triphosphates based on carbon dots: The distance-dependent singlet oxygen trapping

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y. Ding and Q. Jia contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The distance-dependent based sensing mechanism, such as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) absorption of gold nanoparticles, has been used widely in visual detection. In this work, we report another distance-dependent detection method for nucleoside triphosphates (NTPs) based on carbon dots (CDs) (1O2 donor) and 9, 10-diphenylanthracene-2-boronic acid (DABA, 1O2 acceptor). The CDs can generate singlet oxygen (1O2) which allows diffusion within 200 nm. Thus, the distance between CDs and DABA decreased through binding of NTPs (<200 nm), leading to absorption changes of DABA under light irradiation due to 1O2 trapping. This sensing system (CDs@DABA) has high selectivity for the detection of NTPs due to the double molecular recognition and a linear response in the 0–80 μM concentration range was accomplished with the detection limit as low as 4.35 μM.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, carbon dots (CDs) have caused widespread attention among numerous kinds of nanomaterials due to their unique properties and wide ranges of potential technical applications [1,2]. CDs have a large surface area due to their spherical or quasi-spherical structure with a diameter within 10 nm [3]. Given their excellent bio-compatibility, photo-stability, and water dispersability and low cytotoxicity, CDs have been widely used in drug carriers and biosensing and bioimaging applications [4–7]. Extensive efforts have been devoted to the field of chemical sensing to detect heavy metals, including Hg2+ [8], Pb2+ [9], and Cu2+ [10], based on the fluorescence quenching after adding metal ions [11]. Moreover, by enhancing fluorescence or recovering the fluorescence of quenched CDs-metal complexes, CDs were also applied in the detection of S2− and Ag+ due to the formation of more stable species between metal ions and anions [12–14]. In addition, studies have also based on the inner filter effect (IFE) designing CDs-based sensors to achieve ion detection such as CN− and Cr6+ [15,16]. With the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle, CDs were also proposed to be used as immunosensors [17,18]. With the concept of fluorescent quenching, CDs have been applied in detecting some biomacromolecules such as nucleic acid and small bioanalytes such as dopamine and glucose, and some anti-bacterial drugs [19,20].

For in vivo imaging and biosensing, stable and tunable fluorescence emission of CDs is important to achieve photophysical characterization [21]. The generation of singlet oxygen (1O2) under illumination and in the presence of oxygen allows CDs to be applied in photodynamic therapy (PDT) [22]. 1O2 is one kind of reactive oxygen species and can kill cancerous cells [23]. For example, our group synthesized a new kind of CDs with excellent properties, such as positive charge, water dispersibility, photo- and pH stability, and high 1O2 generation ability [24]. The collective properties of the CDs enable them to be highly efficient in vivo PDT of cancer [24]. Although more efforts have been paid to PDT applications, to the best of our knowledge, no sensor using 1O2 production of CDs has been reported.

Generally, 1O2 has a short lifetime in an aqueous solution that allows diffusion within 200 nm [25]. Thus, 1O2 trapping is distance-dependent and can serve as the basis for designing new sensors by tuning the distance between photosensitizer (1O2 donor) and 1O2 trapping agent (1O2 acceptor) [26,27]. As shown in Scheme 1 , when the distance is more than 200 nm, the 1O2 generated by the 1O2 donor decays and cannot react with the 1O2 acceptor. By contrast, when the analyte brings the 1O2 donor and acceptor in close proximity, the reactions are initiated which produce measurable changes in absorption and/or fluorescence spectrum of the 1O2 acceptor. AlphaScreen is a versatile assay technology that uses this principle to screen a broad range of targets and study biomolecular interactions [28]. The distance-dependent 1O2 trapping has high sensitivity and selectivity, however, sensors based on this principle have been hardly reported.

In this work, we selected the CDs photosensitizer as the 1O2 donor and 9, 10-diphenylanthracene-2-boronic acid (DABA) as the 1O2 acceptor to construct a new sensing platform (CDs@DABA) for the detection of nucleoside triphosphates (NTPs). NTPs consisting of three units (a sugar, a nucleobase, and a phosphate residue) play an irreplaceable role in almost all biological events [29–32]. For example, adenosine 5-triphosphate (ATP) is not only significant in providing energy but is an essential component in the metabolism of fats, proteins, sugars and nucleic acids [33–35]. Thus, we selected ATP as the model to confirm our thought. As shown in Scheme 2 , without ATP, the 1O2 generated by CDs can hardly be captured by DABA due to the distance between the CDs and DABA molecular is more than 200 nm. Upon the addition of ATP, the prepared CDs with a high quantum yield of 1O2 have positive charges on the surface, which can interact with phosphate groups in ATP by electrostatic interaction. In addition, boronic acids in DABA can also form stable cyclic borate ester with diols of ATP. Thus, as the 1O2 trapping agent, DABA can react rapidly with 1O2 specifically to form a thermostable endoperoxide accompanied by a decrease in absorbance of DABA. The 1O2 donor and acceptor interact with the phosphate and diols groups in ATP, respectively, to doubly recognize ATP with high specificity.

9, 10-diphenylanthracene-2-boronic acid (DABA) was bought from TCI (Shanghai) Development Co.,Ltd., adenosine 5′-triphosphate disodium salt (ATP) was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd., thymidine 5′-triphosphate sodium salt (TTP), cytidine 5′-triphosphate disodium salt (CTP), guanosine 5′-triphosphate disodium salt (GTP), and uridine 5′-triphosphate trisodium salt (UTP) were bought from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., Ltd., NaCl, MgCl2, Na2HPO4 and other inorganic salts were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical reagent Co., Ltd., HEPES buffer with various pH values were purchased from Beijing Leagene Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All commercial chemicals were used without further purification. The details of the CDs synthesis are provided in our previously report [24].

White light (400–800 nm) was generated from a xenon light source (Solar-500). The intensity of the incident beam was determined by a radiometer (Photoelectric Instrument Factory of Beijing Normal University). STEM image was recorded on a Cs-corrected Titan 80–300 microscope operated at 300 kV. FTIR spectra (4000–400 cm−1) in KBr were gathered on a Varian Excalibur 3100 FTIR spectrometer. Zeta potentials were detected on Zetasize 3000 HS (Malvern, UK). UV–Vis and fluorescence spectra were measured using Hitachi U-3010 and F-4500 spectrophotometers, respectively.

In the experiments, DABA solved in DMSO (2.67 mM) was used as the 1O2-trapping agent while ATP was solved in distilled water (1.8 mM) as the stock solution. For each measurement, 45 μL DABA solution and different amount of ATP solution was added separately to 2 mL of CDs water solution (0.02 mg mL−1). The white light (400–800 nm) with a power density of 8.0 mW cm−2 was employed as the irradiation source. The absorption of DABA at 387 nm was recorded at various irradiation times at room temperature to get the decay rate of the photosensitizing process.

Here we took two groups to detect the absorption changes respectively. One group was used to detect the response of the CDs@DABA to each analyte. The proper amount of each ion solution was added respectively to 2 mL CDs (0.02 mg mL−1) and DABA (2.67 μM, 45 μL) mixture to keep all the final ion concentrations at 60 μM. The other group was used to evaluate the analytical performance of CDs@DABA to ATP in the co-existence of other analyte. The extra 66 μL ATP solution (1.8 mM) was added to the CDs-DABA mixture as mentioned above. Both groups were measured under the same light condition (8.0 mW cm−2) and detected the absorption at 387 nm.

The mixture solution of CDs solution (0.02 mg mL−1) with DABA (60 μM) and ATP (60 μM) was centrifuged after washed by distilled water. Repeated for three times, the CDs@DABA-ATP complex was then measured by FR-IR spectrometer.

The optical properties of CDs are shown in Fig. 1 a. The absorption spectra reveal that it has a broad absorption from 300 nm to 650 nm which indicates that white light is suitable for the production of 1O2. The scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image of the intrinsic crystal structure of CDs with diameters within 10 nm, is presented in Fig. 1b. In addition, the zeta potential of CDs (0.02 mg mL−1) is determined to be +22.6 mV in neutral water (Fig. 1c), allowing the negatively charged ATP to interact with CDs through the electrostatic interaction. The positive surface changes of CDs might be the quaternary ammonium salts, resulting from the carbon source we used in a hydrothermal method. In addition, positively charged CDs were dispersed very well in water and showed good pH stability (Fig. S1).

As shown in Fig. 2 , the absorption of DABA in diluted CDs solution has three main peaks, and the highest peak is at 387 nm. Before the addition of ATP, the distance between the CDs and DABA molecules is more than 200 nm. Therefore, under light irradiation, the 1O2 generated by CDs can hardly be captured by DABA (Scheme 2), leading to slight absorption changes for DABA (Fig. 2a). When a certain amount of ATP was added to the CDs@DABA solution, the absorption value at 387 nm decreased gradually with increasing irradiation time under white light (8 mW cm−2) in 5 min (Fig. 2b). Such a change in the spectrum could be well explained by the ATP-induced aggregation of CDs@DABA-ATP through electrostatic interactions and the formation of stable borate ester, which triggered 1O2 addition reaction with anthracene in DABA.

To investigate more about the relationship between the observed responses and concentrations of ATP, titration experiments were performed (Fig. 3 a). The relative absorption intensity changes increased gradually with the increasing amount of ATP in the concentration ranging from 0 μM to 80 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) at 4.35 μM (3σ/slope), which is comparable to the previously reported sensors with colorimetric or fluorescent detection in Table 1 . Moreover, CDs@DABA can be used in the dual recognition of ATP, resulting in higher selectivity than single electrostatic interaction [43]. CDs@DABA showed similar binding affinity toward other NTPs (TTP, GTP, UTP, CTP) (Fig. 3b), which is due to similar electrostatic interactions and stable boronate complexes. Besides, we have measured the concentration dependence of the absorption change of DABA (10–80 μM) at a given ATP (60 μM). As shown in Fig. S3a, the absorption change increased with increasing concentration of DABA, indicating that the detection is distant sensitive.

The formation of the CDs@DABA-ATP complex was also confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 4 a, compared with the pure CDs sample, many notable vibration bands emerged. The FTIR peaks of the CDs@DABA-ATP complex at approximately 3060 cm−1, which are absent in CDs, are primarily attributed to C-H stretching vibration of the anthracene portion of DABA. The apparent featured peaks at approximately 1620 cm−1 and 1271 cm−1 are assigned to C=N and C-N stretching vibration respectively that originate from the special structure in ATP molecule of Purine. The absorption band at around 1072 cm−1 is assigned to C-O symmetric vibrations in ATP. The peaks at about 1400 cm−1, 720 cm−1 and 690 cm−1 are mainly identical to the IR peaks of DABA, due to the conjugated effect as well as the C-H surface bending vibration of benzene ring. There are many overlapping peaks between CDs@DABA-ATP and DABA because the complex is likely to form a core-shell mode with DABA surrounding the surface while CDs are inside. Moreover, the hydrodynamic sizes and the zeta potential of CDs (0.02 mg mL−1) as well as CDs@DABA-ATP complex were measured (Fig. 1). The size distribution changed enormously, approximately from 47.05 nm (Fig. 1c) to 216.7 nm (Fig. 1d), in the present of DABA (60 μM) and ATP (60 μM) in CDs solution (0.02 mg mL−1). The zeta potential of CDs@DABA-ATP was shown in Fig. 1f. Comparing the zeta potential of CDs at +22.6 mV (Fig. 1e), the introduction of DABA (60 μM) and ATP (60 μM) led to the decrease of zeta potential at approximately +4.91 mV, which also revealed the form of the CDs@DABA-ATP. As shown in Fig. S2, the zeta potential only slightly decreased upon the addition of 100 μM ATP comparing that of 80 μM ATP, which might indicate the complete surface coverage of CDs (0.02 mg mL−1).

To investigate the effect of pH, the CDs@DABA was dispersed in HEPES with different pH values in the absence and presence of ATP. The absorption intensity of CDs@DABA at 387 nm did not change obviously in a wide pH range from 4 to 9 (Fig. 4b). In addition, CDs@DABA had good response towards ATP ranging from pH 5.0 to pH 9.0, demonstrating that CDs@DABA can be used to detect ATP in the physiological pH range (from 6.0 to 8.0). To assess the possibility of the CDs@DABA-ATP for specific sensing of ATP, the absorption responses among numerous kinds of analytes were subsequently performed. ADP and AMP are the main metabolites of ATP, thus we investigated first their interference in ATP detection. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the corresponding absorption spectra proved that CDs@DABA exhibited high selectivity for ATP over the other two competitors. The different numbers of phosphate groups in the three compounds might have affected the binding ability to CDs. For further examination of the selectivity toward ATP, a number of common cations (Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+) and anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, AcO−, NO3 −, H2PO4 −, HSO3 −, CO3 2−, HPO4 2−, P2O7 4-) were investigated. Based on the results, ATP induced the most significant absorption changes of CDs@DABA, whereas the other cations and anions showed slight changes on the absorption (Fig. 5b). Competition experiments were also carried out and the presence of common ions created small or no interference to detect ATP, demonstrating its good selectivity and sensitivity.

We also investigated whether the ion strength affected the result by adding various contents of NaCl (0–1000 μM) to the CDs@DABA system, and the results proved that the ion strength had little effect on the result at low ionic concentration (<800 μM) (Fig. S3b). For further investigation of the possible application of our detecting method, we performed the ATP detection in 1% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The results were shown in Fig. 6 and the LOD was determined to be 2.28 μM (3 σ/slope) with the ATP concentration from 0 to 80 μM. This reflected that this system had the potential use in more complicated conditions.

In conclusion, we presented a new sensing strategy inspired by the distance-dependent 1O2 trapping. By using CDs as 1O2 donor and DABA as 1O2 acceptor, we have successfully developed an efficient sensing platform (CDs@DABA) for the selective detection of NTPs by the double molecular recognition. This work provides a novel design thinking for design of sensors based on the distance-dependent 1O2 trapping sensor. Investigations along these lines for the development of more 1O2 donor-acceptor-based systems for recognition of small molecules, especially sensitive detection using fluorescence, are in progress in our laboratory.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 61720106014 and 21673265) and Key Research Program of Frontier Sciences, CAS (Grant NO. QYZDJ-SSW-JSC032).

Cross-coupling reactions of nucleoside triphosphates followed by polymerase incorporation. Construction and applications of base-functionalized nucleic acids

Poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-N-methylolacrylamide-co-ethylene dimethacrylate) monolith coupled to high-performance liquid chromatography for the determination of adenosine phosphates in royal jelly
Electrochemical aptamer-based nanosensor fabricated on single Au nanowire electrodes for adenosine triphosphate assay

Highly sensitive surface-enhanced Raman scattering detection of adenosine triphosphate based on core–satellite assemblies
Colorimetric and visual determination of adenosine triphosphate using a boronic acid as the recognition element, and based on the deaggregation of gold nanoparticles
The aptamer DNA-templated fluorescence silver nanoclusters: ATP detection and preliminary mechanism investigation

A ratiometric fluorescent probe with unexpected high selectivity for ATP and its application in cell imaging
Turn-on fluorescence probes based on pyranine/viologen charge-transfer complexes for the determination of nucleotides
